title: Optimize Your Next.js App: From 3s to 0.8s Load Time
excerpt: Practical techniques I used to dramatically improve performance — lazy loading, image optimization, code splitting, and edge caching strategies.
content: ## The Problem When I first deployed my Next.js application, the initial load time was hovering around 3 seconds. For a modern web app, that's unacceptable — users expect pages to load in under a second. ## Step 1: Image Optimization The first and most impactful change was optimizing images. I switched all `` tags to Next.js `` component, which provides: - **Automatic WebP/AVIF conversion** — serving modern formats to supported browsers - **Lazy loading by default** — images below the fold don't block initial render - **Responsive sizing** — serving appropriately sized images for each device This alone cut load time by nearly 40%. ## Step 2: Code Splitting with Dynamic Imports Next.js supports dynamic imports out of the box. I identified heavy components that weren't needed on initial render and loaded them lazily: ```tsx const HeavyChart = dynamic(() => import('./HeavyChart'), { loading: () => , ssr: false, }); ``` ## Step 3: Edge Caching with CDN By moving static assets to a CDN and configuring proper cache headers, I reduced server response times dramatically. Combined with ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration), most pages are served from the edge. ## Step 4: Bundle Analysis Using `@next/bundle-analyzer`, I discovered several large dependencies that could be replaced with lighter alternatives: - Replaced **moment.js** (290KB) with **dayjs** (2KB) - Replaced **lodash** (72KB) with individual imports - Tree-shook unused icon imports ## Results After applying all optimizations: - **First Contentful Paint**: 3.2s → 0.6s - **Largest Contentful Paint**: 3.8s → 0.8s - **Time to Interactive**: 4.1s → 1.2s - **Lighthouse Score**: 54 → 98 The key takeaway? Performance optimization isn't about one big change — it's about systematically identifying and eliminating bottlenecks.

title: Building a Real-time Dashboard with Firebase & React
excerpt: A step-by-step guide to creating live data dashboards using Firestore listeners, React hooks, and Recharts for beautiful data visualization.

title: Why I Switched from REST to GraphQL (And When You Shouldn't)
excerpt: My honest experience migrating a production API to GraphQL — the wins, the pain points, and the cases where REST is still the better choice.
content:
## The Motivation

Our REST API had grown to 47 endpoints. The mobile team was making 6-8 requests per screen. Over-fetching was rampant — we were sending 10x more data than clients needed.

## The Migration Process

### Phase 1: Schema Design

We started by mapping our REST resources to GraphQL types:

```graphql
type User {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  email: String!
  posts: [Post!]!
  followers: [User!]!
}

type Post {
  id: ID!
  title: String!
  content: String!
  author: User!
  comments: [Comment!]!
}
```

### Phase 2: Resolver Implementation

Each field resolver was connected to our existing data layer:

```typescript
const resolvers = {
  User: {
    posts: (parent) => PostService.getByUserId(parent.id),
    followers: (parent) => UserService.getFollowers(parent.id),
  },
};
```

### Phase 3: Client Migration

We used Apollo Client on the frontend, replacing multiple fetch calls with single queries:

```graphql
query DashboardData {
  currentUser {
    name
    avatar
    notifications { id, message, read }
  }
  recentPosts(limit: 5) {
    title, excerpt, publishedAt
  }
}
```

## The Wins

- **Reduced network requests by 73%** — one query replaces multiple REST calls
- **Payload size dropped 60%** — clients request exactly what they need
- **Faster feature development** — frontend can iterate without backend changes
- **Self-documenting API** — GraphQL introspection replaces API docs

## The Pain Points

- **N+1 query problem** — required DataLoader for efficient batching
- **Caching complexity** — REST's HTTP caching is simpler than GraphQL cache strategies
- **Learning curve** — team needed 2-3 weeks to become productive
- **File uploads** — still awkward in GraphQL, we kept a REST endpoint for this

## When REST is Better

Don't switch to GraphQL if:
- Your API is simple with few endpoints
- You rely heavily on HTTP caching
- Your team is small and REST works fine
- You're building a CRUD-heavy admin panel

## Conclusion

GraphQL was the right choice for our use case — a complex app with diverse clients needing different data shapes. But it's not a silver bullet. Choose the tool that fits your specific needs.
